Bumps [redis](https://github.com/redis/node-redis) from 4.7.1 to 6.0.1.
Release notes

Sourced from redis's releases.

redis@6.0.1

What's Changed

  • ci: bump GitHub Actions to Node 24-compatible versions by @​nkaradzhov in redis/node-redis#3298
  • chore(tests): bump default docker test image to 8.8.0 by @​nkaradzhov in redis/node-redis#3297
  • fix(client): align RedisClientOptions RESP default with RedisClientType by @​aartisonigra in redis/node-redis#3303
  • test(client): fix three flaky tests around socket/connect lifecycle by @​nkaradzhov in redis/node-redis#3299
  • fix: embed sources in emitted sourcemaps by @​raashish1601 in redis/node-redis#3274
  • Sentinel scan iterator by @​harshrai654 in redis/node-redis#3141
  • ci: replace brittle Codecov uploader with codecov-action by @​nkaradzhov in redis/node-redis#3314
  • Fix/issue 3311 by @​aartisonigra in redis/node-redis#3313
  • fix(sentinel): preserve seed nodes in sentinelRootNodes after topology update (#3237) by @​aartisonigra in redis/node-redis#3306

New Contributors

  • @​aartisonigra made their first contribution in redis/node-redis#3303
  • @​harshrai654 made their first contribution in redis/node-redis#3141

Full Changelog: https://github.com/redis/node-redis/compare/redis@6.0.0...redis@6.0.1

redis@6.0.0

Changes

This is the first major release of node-redis since 5.x. The headline change is RESP3 by default, alongside broader Redis 8.8 command coverage, pubsub/cluster reliability fixes, and a bumped minimum Node.js version.

Key changes

  • RESP3 is the default protocol — opt back into RESP2 explicitly if you need the old wire format
  • Node.js 20 is the new minimum supported runtime
  • Redis 8.8 coverage — new array commands, INCREX / INCREXBYFLOAT, ZINTER/ZUNION COUNT aggregator, XNACK, CLIENT UNBLOCK
  • Sentinel & cluster pubsub fixes for failover-moved connections and sharded topology recovery
  • Stack modules exposed on pool type and the core client classes are now publicly re-exported

🔥 Breaking Changes

See the v5 → v6 migration guide for migration steps.

  • RESP3 is now the default protocol (#3215)
  • Node.js 20 is now the minimum supported version (#3293)
  • New default values for keepAliveInitialDelay (30s) and commandTimeout (5s) (#3292)

🔒 Security

  • entraid: bump @azure/msal-node to 5.x to drop vulnerable transitive uuid (CVE-2026-41907) (#3269)

🚀 New Features

  • Add CLIENT UNBLOCK command (#3266)
  • Add XNACK command with options (#3238)
  • Add Redis 8.8 array commands (#3285)
  • Add INCREX and INCREXBYFLOAT commands (#3288)
  • Add COUNT aggregator to ZINTER / ZINTERSTORE / ZUNION / ZUNIONSTORE (#3243)
  • Add FPHA option to JSON.SET (#3235)

... (truncated)

body:
Bumps [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) from 1.16.1 to 1.18.1.
Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.18.1 — June 21, 2026

This release focuses on Node HTTP adapter fixes, safer AxiosError serialisation, runtime/type correctness fixes, documentation updates, and dependency maintenance.

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • AxiosError Serialisation: Made AxiosError#cause non-enumerable to prevent circular JSON serialisation failures when errors include nested causes. (#10913)
  • Node HTTP Adapter: Guarded socket.setKeepAlive for proxy agent streams, accepted path-only URLs when socketPath is configured, deferred environment proxy handling to Node, and explicitly passed maxBodyLength through to follow-redirects. (#10917, #10930, #10942, #10993)
  • Runtime and Type Correctness: Fixed several runtime crashes, type definition mismatches, and incorrect error handling paths. (#10959, #11021)
  • AxiosURLSearchParams: Switched the encoder callback to an arrow function so encoder.call(this) receives the AxiosURLSearchParams instance correctly. (#11019)

🔧 Maintenance & Chores

  • Documentation: Documented sensitive headers and status transition behaviour, prepared cleaned-up docs, added Deno install instructions, and clarified that request data is request-specific (#11007, #11010, #11023, #11025)

  • Dependencies: Bumped vite, rollup, form-data, js-yaml, and multer across the root project, docs, smoke tests, and module test workspaces. (#11011, #11012, #11013, #11014, #11015, #11016, #11017, #11026)

🌟 New Contributors

We are thrilled to welcome our new contributors. Thank you for helping improve axios:

  • @​webdevelopersrinu (#10913)
  • @​sijie-Z (#10993)
  • @​bartlomieju (#11023)
  • @​JSap0914 (#11019)

Full Changelog

v1.18.0 — June 13, 2026

This release hardens redirect and URL handling, improves the validateStatus configuration semantics, and includes updates to documentation, dependencies, and release metadata.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Redirect Header Safety: Added Node HTTP adapter support for stripping caller-specified sensitive headers on cross-origin redirects, helping prevent custom auth headers such as API keys from leaking to another origin. (#10892)

  • URL And Request Hardening: Rejects malformed http: and https: URLs that omit // with ERR_INVALID_URL, while tightening prototype-pollution-safe config reads, stream size limits, FormData depth handling, data URL sizing, and local NO_PROXY matching. (#11000)

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • Status Validation: Added transitional.validateStatusUndefinedResolves so applications can opt in to treating validateStatus: undefined like the option was omitted, while validateStatus: null remains the explicit way to accept every status. (#10899)

🔧 Maintenance & Chores

  • Documentation: Published the v1.17.0 release notes, fixed a changelog typo, clarified the package update PR policy, and marked the proxy request config as Node.js-only in the advanced docs. (#10984, #10988, #10992, #10995)

  • Dependencies: Bumped @babel/core, @babel/preset-env, @commitlint/cli, @commitlint/config-conventional, @rollup/plugin-babel, @rollup/plugin-commonjs, @vitest/browser, @vitest/browser-playwright, eslint, lint-staged, rollup, vitest, and actions/checkout. (#10989, #10996, #10997)

  • Release Metadata: Prepared the 1.18.0 release by updating package metadata and the runtime VERSION value. (#11003)

... (truncated)

Sourced from axios's changelog.

Changelog

v1.18.0 — June 13, 2026

This release hardens redirect and URL handling, improves the validateStatus configuration semantics, and includes updates to documentation, dependencies, and release metadata.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Redirect Header Safety: Added Node HTTP adapter support for stripping caller-specified sensitive headers on cross-origin redirects, helping prevent custom auth headers such as API keys from leaking to another origin. (#10892)

  • URL And Request Hardening: Rejects malformed http: and https: URLs that omit // with ERR_INVALID_URL, while tightening prototype-pollution-safe config reads, stream size limits, FormData depth handling, data URL sizing, and local NO_PROXY matching. (#11000)

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • Status Validation: Added transitional.validateStatusUndefinedResolves so applications can opt in to treating validateStatus: undefined like the option was omitted, while validateStatus: null remains the explicit way to accept every status. (#10899)

body:
Bumps [numpy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) from 1.26.2 to 2.5.0.
Release notes

Sourced from numpy's releases.

v2.5.0 (June 21, 2026)

NumPy 2.5.0 Release Notes

Numpy 2.5.0 is a transitional release. It drops support for Python 3.11, marking the end of distutils, and expires a large number of deprecations made in the 2.0.x release. It also improves free threading and brings sorting into compliance with the array-api standard with the addition of descending sorts. There is also a fair amount of preparation for Python 3.15, which will be supported starting with the first rc.

This release supports Python versions 3.12-3.14.

Highlights

  • Distutils has been removed,
  • Many expired deprecations, see below,
  • Many new deprecations, see below,
  • Many static typing improvements.
  • Improved support for free threading,
  • Support for descending sorts,

See New Features below for other additions.

Deprecations

  • numpy.char.chararray is deprecated. Use an ndarray with a string or bytes dtype instead.

    (gh-30605)

  • numpy.take now correctly checks if the result can be cast to the provided out=out under the same-kind rule. A DeprecationWarning is given now when this check fails. Previously, take incorrectly checked if out could be cast to the result (the wrong direction). This deprecation also affects compress and possibly other functions. (Future versions of NumPy may tighten the casting check further.)

    (gh-30615)

  • The numpy.char.[as]array functions are deprecated. Use an numpy.[as]array with a string or bytes dtype instead.

    (gh-30802)

  • Setting the dtype attribute is deprecated because mutating an array is unsafe if an array is shared, especially by multiple threads. As an alternative, you can create a view with a new dtype via array.view(dtype=new_dtype).

    (gh-29244)

... (truncated)

Changelog

Sourced from numpy's changelog.

This is a walkthrough of the NumPy 2.4.0 release on Linux, which will be the first feature release using the numpy/numpy-release <https://github.com/numpy/numpy-release>__ repository.

The commands can be copied into the command line, but be sure to replace 2.4.0 with the correct version. This should be read together with the :ref:general release guide <prepare_release>.

Facility preparation

Before beginning to make a release, use the requirements/*_requirements.txt files to ensure that you have the needed software. Most software can be installed with pip, but some will require apt-get, dnf, or whatever your system uses for software. You will also need a GitHub personal access token (PAT) to push the documentation. There are a few ways to streamline things:

  • Git can be set up to use a keyring to store your GitHub personal access token. Search online for the details.

Prior to release

Add/drop Python versions

When adding or dropping Python versions, multiple config and CI files need to be edited in addition to changing the minimum version in pyproject.toml. Make these changes in an ordinary PR against main and backport if necessary. We currently release wheels for new Python versions after the first Python RC once manylinux and cibuildwheel support that new Python version.

Backport pull requests

Changes that have been marked for this release must be backported to the maintenance/2.4.x branch.

Update 2.4.0 milestones

Look at the issues/prs with 2.4.0 milestones and either push them off to a later version, or maybe remove the milestone. You may need to add a milestone.

Check the numpy-release repo

title: Security: Email Enumeration via Password Reset OTP Verification Endpoint
body:
Fixed email enumeration in POST /api/auth/verify-otp by hardening verifyPasswordResetOtp() in backend/src/controllers/auth.controller.js.

Before: if email existed but OTP was invalid/expired, the endpoint returned OTP-specific error responses; if email didn’t exist it returned success—allowing attackers to infer whether an email is registered.
After: the endpoint now always returns the same generic success response (success: true with message "If the provided information is valid, verification succeeded."), regardless of:
whether the email exists
whether the OTP is valid/invalid
whether the OTP is expired or attempts exceeded


closes #879

title: [FEATURE]: Implement Client-Side Password Entropy Evaluation Meter and Cryptographic Salt Generator
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem?

Currently, when generating or customizing credentials through the AI layout, the client interface accepts varying string lengths without analyzing structural complexity or entropy scores on the fly. If an AI pipeline yields short, predictable phrase loops, or if a user inputs weak phrases, the system accepts it without local validation metrics. Relying solely on remote API formatting leaves generated keys vulnerable to credential dictionary attacks.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Introduce an asynchronous validation utility that dynamically calculates bits of entropy and integrates an optional client-side cryptographic salting configuration prior to local database caching or clipboard actions.

1. **Entropy Metric Calculation:** Integrate a clean, mathematical entropy evaluator inside the creation component using standard Shannon Entropy calculations:

$$E = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \log_2 P_i$$



*(or utilizing lightweight zxcvbn evaluation rules)* to categorize passwords dynamically into structural states (Weak, Medium, Strong).
2. **Dynamic UI Feedback:** Append an active multi-tiered progress metric meter right beneath password generation panels that smoothly transitions across colors (Red/Yellow/Green) depending on safety scores.
3. **Local Cryptographic Salting Wrapper:** Add an advanced feature toggle allowing users to append a high-entropy client-side salt generated via `crypto.getRandomValues()` before secrets pass over to persistence logic blocks.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Instead of local estimation modules, the app could push strings to a third-party scoring service API endpoint. However, transmitting unencrypted password states over the wire just for validation defeats the core privacy model of a secure credentials repository.

### Additional Context

UI Mockups / Screenshots (if applicable):

[x] Before (Current UI - Password string displays as plain raw text inside standard fields with no visual score indicators)

[ ] After (Proposed UI mockup - An entry meter bar changes states dynamically and provides a toggle button for appending cryptographic client salts)

title: fix: "Process with AI" Fails to Generate Result and Shows 404 Error
body:
Implemented a robust fallback for the API base URL:

Updated frontend/src/services/api.jsx to use:
javascript
const apiBaseUrl =
  import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL ??
  (import.meta.env.DEV ? 'http://localhost:5005/api' : '/api');
This ensures the frontend correctly targets the backend API even when VITE_API_URL is missing, preventing the 404 error on “Process with AI”.

title: [FEATURE] ELUSoC_2026: Implement Asynchronous Client-Side Magic Bytes Signature Verification
body:
# Problem
Users can rename arbitrary files (e.g. `.txt` to `.jpg`) and upload them, wasting network bandwidth and causing processing errors on the server.

# Current Behavior
Only extension and mime-type strings are verified.

# Why This Improvement Is Needed
Verifying actual file headers (magic numbers) on the client side blocks invalid formats before upload.

# Proposed Solution
Implement `validateImageMagicBytes` using `FileReader` to read file headers and confirm JPEG (`FFD8FF`), PNG (`89504E47`), or WEBP signature patterns.

# Expected Outcome
Immediate feedback on invalid uploads, preventing backend load.

# Additional Notes
Runs asynchronously.

title: ELUSoC_2026: Harden CORS Origin Matching and Limit Request Headers
body:
# Problem
The current CORS policy allows wildcard origins alongside credentials flags (`credentials: true`), which violates standard browser security and throws security warnings.

# Current Behavior
Returns `true` for wildcards when `credentials` is true.

# Why This Improvement Is Needed
To secure HTTP resource sharing and resolve browser validation errors.

# Proposed Solution
Update `cors` configuration in `app.js` to echo the incoming request origin for wildcards when credentials mode is enabled.

# Expected Outcome
Secure and compliant CORS response headers.

# Additional Notes
Restrict allowed methods and request headers to minimize exposure.

title: ELUSoC_2026: Harden Input Sanitization Middleware against Prototype Pollution
body:
# Problem
The current input sanitization middleware recursively traverses incoming requests but does not guard against prototype pollution keys (`__proto__`, `constructor`, `prototype`). This can lead to server crashes or properties override.

# Current Behavior
Recursive cleaner processes all keys without prototype checks.

# Why This Improvement Is Needed
To safeguard the Node.js/Express process from prototype pollution exploits.

# Proposed Solution
Modify `cleanHtmlTags` to intercept and delete prototype keys during traversal.

# Expected Outcome
Hardened middleware that drops prototype-polluting payloads.

# Additional Notes
None.

title: All routes show the same document title and meta tags regardless of page
body:
## Problem

The `index.html` has static OG tags with a single title ("SnapPass AI - Passport Photo Studio") and description for all routes. The SPA has 10+ pages (upload, editor, print-preview, studio, compare-requirements, history, terms, privacy) but they all show the same document title in browser tabs. Sharing any page on social media shows identical metadata.

## Proposed fix

Create a lightweight `useDocumentMeta` hook that updates `document.title` and meta tags per-route. No heavy library needed since Vite doesn't do SSR.

```jsx
useDocumentMeta({
  title: "Editor - SnapPass AI",
  description: "Edit your passport photo with AI background removal"
});
```

## Files affected
- New: `frontend/src/hooks/useDocumentMeta.js`
- `frontend/src/pages/*.jsx` (add hook call to each page)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Each route has a unique document title visible in browser tabs
- [ ] Meta description updates per-route for better SEO
- [ ] OG tags update for social sharing (best effort without SSR)

title: Backend crashes on startup when Redis is unavailable instead of graceful degradation
body:
## Problem

Redis is imported as a hard dependency and used in `cache.middleware.js`. The `envCheck.js` validates MONGO_URI, JWT_SECRET, and other vars but NOT Redis. If Redis is unavailable (common in local dev when someone doesn't have Redis installed), the backend crashes on startup with an unhandled connection error.

## Proposed fix

1. Make Redis optional with graceful degradation (skip caching when unavailable)
2. Add connection error handling with retry logic in `redis.js`
3. If Redis is down, the cache middleware should pass through to the handler directly
4. Add Redis status to the health check endpoint

## Files affected
- `backend/src/config/redis.js`
- `backend/src/middleware/cache.middleware.js`
- Health check endpoint (optional)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Backend starts successfully even when Redis is not running
- [ ] Cache middleware degrades gracefully (requests pass through without caching)
- [ ] Redis reconnects automatically when it becomes available
- [ ] Console warns clearly that caching is disabled

title: Editor page controls lack keyboard accessibility and ARIA attributes
body:
## Problem

The editor page has interactive controls (color pickers, background selector swatches, crop handles, quantity inputs) that lack keyboard accessibility. Users relying on screen readers or keyboard-only navigation cannot operate the photo editor.

Specific gaps:
- Background color swatches have no `role="radio"` or `aria-label`
- QuantityInput likely missing `aria-valuemin`/`aria-valuemax`/`aria-valuenow`
- Crop handle interactions are mouse-only with no keyboard equivalents

## Proposed fix

Add ARIA attributes and keyboard event handlers to interactive editor components. Follow WAI-ARIA patterns for radio groups (color selection) and spinbuttons (quantity).

## Files affected
- `frontend/src/components/BackgroundSelector.jsx`
- `frontend/src/components/QuantityInput.jsx`
- `frontend/src/pages/EditorPage.jsx`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Background selector navigable with arrow keys and announced by screen readers
- [ ] Quantity inputs usable with keyboard (up/down arrows)
- [ ] All interactive elements have appropriate ARIA labels

title: Python Flask service accepts unlimited requests without rate limiting or payload size check
body:
## Problem

The Flask AI service at port 8000 has zero rate limiting. The Express backend uses `express-rate-limit` on all API routes, but the Python service is unprotected. If it's accidentally exposed (Docker port mapping, reverse proxy misconfiguration), an attacker can flood it with heavy image processing requests causing CPU exhaustion.

Also, `MAX_FILE_MB` is configured in `config.py` but never enforced in Flask route handlers.

## Proposed fix

1. Add `flask-limiter` with a reasonable cap (e.g., 10 requests/minute per IP for image processing)
2. Check `request.content_length` against `MAX_FILE_MB` before processing
3. Return 429 with a clear message when rate limited

## Files affected
- `python-ai-service/main.py`
- `python-ai-service/config.py`
- `python-ai-service/requirements.txt` (add flask-limiter)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Image processing endpoints enforce per-IP rate limits
- [ ] Oversized payloads are rejected before processing starts
- [ ] Rate limit responses include Retry-After header

title: feat: Add client-side image preview and dimension/file-size validation before AI API submission
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Yes. Currently, when a user selects or uploads an image for AI processing, there is no immediate visual confirmation (preview) or feedback showing the image details before submission. Furthermore, if a user uploads an extremely large file, it goes directly to the backend/API, which can waste server bandwidth, hit API payload limits, or cause unexpected timeouts without warning the user beforehand.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I would like to implement a robust client-side validation and instant preview workflow:
1. **Instant Preview:** As soon as a user selects an image, generate a local object URL to display a clean preview of the selected image.
2. **File Metrics:** Display basic metadata of the chosen file beneath the preview, such as the File Name, Format (e.g., JPEG, PNG), and File Size (e.g., 1.5 MB).
3. **Client-side Constraint:** Add a file size safety threshold (e.g., maximum 5MB). If the file exceeds this limit, show a user-friendly error message or toast and disable the processing button to prevent redundant API calls.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
An alternative would be letting the backend handle all image validations and error returns, but this degrades the user experience by introducing unnecessary latency and wasting server resources on invalid payloads.

**Additional context**
This feature will enhance the UI/UX, optimize the application's performance, and prevent unnecessary API failures by capturing invalid files directly in the browser.

title: [Bug]: README Documents Both Frontend and Backend on Port 3000 — Guaranteed Port Conflict for Every Local Setup
body:
## 🐛 Critical Setup Bug: Frontend and Backend Assigned the Same Port

### What I Found

The Getting Started section of the README instructs contributors to run:

**Frontend:**
cd frontend

npm install

npm start            ← runs on http://localhost:3000/

**Backend:**
cd backend

npm install

npm run dev          ← also documented as http://localhost:3000/

Both services are documented to bind to **port 3000 simultaneously**. 
This is a guaranteed port conflict — whichever service starts 
second will either crash or silently fail.

### The Contradiction Gets Worse

The Docker Compose section of the same README assigns completely 
different ports:

| Service  | Docker Port | Local Dev (README) |
|---|---|---|
| Frontend | `localhost:5173` | `localhost:3000` |
| Backend  | `localhost:5000` | `localhost:3000` |
| Python AI | `localhost:8000` | `localhost:8000` |

So Docker uses `5173` for frontend and `5000` for backend — 
the correct, non-conflicting setup — but the manual local dev 
instructions document both on `3000`.

The backend `.env` example also sets `CORS_ORIGIN=http://localhost:3000` 
and `PORT=3000`, meaning if the backend correctly runs on `3000`, 
the frontend env variable `VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3000/api` 
would point to itself (since Vite's default is `5173`, not `3000`).

### Impact
Every contributor who follows the README's Getting Started 
section step-by-step cannot run the full stack locally. 
They hit a port conflict before writing a single line of code.

### What the Correct Ports Should Be
Based on the Docker configuration and Vite defaults:
- Frontend: `http://localhost:5173` (Vite default)
- Backend: `http://localhost:5000`
- Python AI Service: `http://localhost:8000`
- `VITE_API_URL` should be `http://localhost:5000/api`

### Second Bug Found While Investigating

There is also a `dummy.jpg` file committed to the repository 
root — clearly a development test artifact. It has no place 
in a production repository root and should either be moved 
to `frontend/public/test-assets/` or added to `.gitignore`.

### I'd like to work on this (GSSoC'26)
Will submit a PR correcting the port documentation in 
`README.md`, aligning `frontend/.env.example` with the 
correct backend port, and removing/relocating `dummy.jpg`.
Labels: bug, documentation, good first issue, GSSoC'26

title: Enhancement: Robust Environment
body:
**Description**
Currently, the backend scaffold for SnapPass-AI is ready for controller logic implementation. However, the environment variable validation script (validateEnv.js) relies on basic manual string checks (or lacks a strict schema validation layer). Manual checking often leads to uncaught runtime errors if a variable is missing or formatted incorrectly (e.g., PORT being passed as a string instead of a number, or missing a required string like MONGO_URI).
Upgrading this to a schema-driven validation approach ensures the application fails immediately and cleanly at startup with descriptive error messages if the environment is misconfigured.

🎯** Expected Behavior**
The backend should automatically validate all required environment variables at boot time using a strict schema parser (like zod or joi) and throw a clean, human-readable error list if anything is missing or type-mismatched.

🛠️** Proposed Solution / Corrected Code**

1. Add Validation Dependency
Add a lightweight, standard validation library like zod to the backend package.json dependencies.

2. Refactored validateEnv.js
Replace manual if (!process.env.VAR) checks with a strict schema logic block

GsSoc 2026.

title: Bug: POST /api/upload, /api/process, and /api/print/generate-sheet have no authentication
body:
## Bug Description
Three critical routes lack `authMiddleware`:
- `POST /api/upload` (upload.routes.js:14)
- `POST /api/process` (image.routes.js:12)
- `POST /api/print/generate-sheet` (print.routes.js:13)

Anyone can upload files, trigger AI processing, or generate print sheets without being logged in.

## Impact
- Unauthenticated file uploads can exhaust disk space
- Unauthenticated AI processing can exhaust compute resources
- Potential abuse for processing arbitrary files through the AI pipeline

## Suggested Fix
Add `authMiddleware` to all three routes:
```javascript
router.post('/upload', authMiddleware, upload.single('image'), uploadPhoto);
router.post('/process', authMiddleware, processImage);
router.post('/generate-sheet', authMiddleware, generatePrintSheet);
```

## Labels
`bug`, `security`, `good first issue`

title: Bug: POST /api/face-quality returns raw exception details via str(e)
body:
## Bug Description
The `face_quality_check()` function in `python-ai-service/main.py:107` returns `str(e)` in its 500 error response, leaking internal implementation details to clients.

```python
except Exception as e:
    return JSONResponse(status_code=500, content={"error": str(e)})
```

## Impact
Information disclosure: attackers can learn about internal file paths, library versions, or database structure from error messages.

## Suggested Fix
Replace `str(e)` with a generic error message:
```python
except Exception as e:
    return JSONResponse(status_code=500, content={"error": "Face quality check failed. Please try again."})
```

## Labels
`bug`, `good first issue`

v1.18.0 — June 13, 2026

This release hardens redirect and URL handling, improves the validateStatus configuration semantics, and includes updates to documentation, dependencies, and release metadata.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Redirect Header Safety: Added Node HTTP adapter support for stripping caller-specified sensitive headers on cross-origin redirects, helping prevent custom auth headers such as API keys from leaking to another origin. (#10892)

  • URL And Request Hardening: Rejects malformed http: and https: URLs that omit // with ERR_INVALID_URL, while tightening prototype-pollution-safe config reads, stream size limits, FormData depth handling, data URL sizing, and local NO_PROXY matching. (#11000)

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • Status Validation: Added transitional.validateStatusUndefinedResolves so applications can opt in to treating validateStatus: undefined like the option was omitted, while validateStatus: null remains the explicit way to accept every status. (#10899)

body:
Bumps [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) from 1.16.1 to 1.18.0.
Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.18.0 — June 13, 2026

This release hardens redirect and URL handling, improves the validateStatus configuration semantics, and includes updates to documentation, dependencies, and release metadata.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Redirect Header Safety: Added Node HTTP adapter support for stripping caller-specified sensitive headers on cross-origin redirects, helping prevent custom auth headers such as API keys from leaking to another origin. (#10892)

  • URL And Request Hardening: Rejects malformed http: and https: URLs that omit // with ERR_INVALID_URL, while tightening prototype-pollution-safe config reads, stream size limits, FormData depth handling, data URL sizing, and local NO_PROXY matching. (#11000)

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • Status Validation: Added transitional.validateStatusUndefinedResolves so applications can opt in to treating validateStatus: undefined like the option was omitted, while validateStatus: null remains the explicit way to accept every status. (#10899)

🔧 Maintenance & Chores

  • Documentation: Published the v1.17.0 release notes, fixed a changelog typo, clarified the package update PR policy, and marked the proxy request config as Node.js-only in the advanced docs. (#10984, #10988, #10992, #10995)

  • Dependencies: Bumped @babel/core, @babel/preset-env, @commitlint/cli, @commitlint/config-conventional, @rollup/plugin-babel, @rollup/plugin-commonjs, @vitest/browser, @vitest/browser-playwright, eslint, lint-staged, rollup, vitest, and actions/checkout. (#10989, #10996, #10997)

  • Release Metadata: Prepared the 1.18.0 release by updating package metadata and the runtime VERSION value. (#11003)

🌟 New Contributors

We are thrilled to welcome our new contributors. Thank you for helping improve axios:

  • @​drori12 (#10984)
  • @​eyupcanakman (#10899)
  • @​Adi-Beker (#10995)

Full Changelog

v1.17.0 — June 1, 2026

This release adds Node HTTP zstd decompression, hardens config and release workflows, and fixes authentication, header, proxy, and type-handling regressions.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Config Hardening: Guarded socketPath, params, and paramsSerializer reads with own-property checks to prevent inherited prototype values from affecting request behavior, including SSRF-sensitive paths. (#10901, #10922)
  • Release Publishing: Switched the publish workflow to npm staged publishing for safer, auditable package releases with provenance. (#10926)

🚀 New Features

  • HTTP Compression: Added Node HTTP adapter support for zstd response decompression, with transitional.advertiseZstdAcceptEncoding controlling whether zstd is advertised in Accept-Encoding. (#6792, #10920)

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • Authentication Handling: Restored Basic auth on same-origin Node redirects while continuing to strip credentials cross-origin, and aligned the fetch adapter with HTTP adapter behavior for URL-embedded Basic auth. (#10929, #10896)
  • Proxy TLS: Preserved user httpsAgent TLS options when tunneling HTTPS requests through HTTP CONNECT proxies. (#10957)
  • React Native FormData: Cleared default Content-Type for React Native FormData so multipart boundaries can be generated correctly. (#10898)

title: [FEATURE] Add FAQ Page and Footer Navigation Link
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently, SnapPass AI does not provide a dedicated FAQ page to address common user questions regarding passport photo generation, AI processing, supported country standards, privacy, downloads and print-ready outputs.

Users visiting the platform may be unfamiliar with passport photo requirements or how the AI processing pipeline works. Without a centralized FAQ section, users may need to contact support or leave the website before understanding the platform's capabilities and limitations.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I would like to add a dedicated FAQ page accessible through the footer navigation.

The page should be:

* Fully responsive across desktop, tablet, and mobile devices.
* Compatible with both Light and Dark modes.
* Designed using accordion/collapsible FAQ cards for better readability.
* Consistent with the existing UI and design system.

Suggested FAQ entries:

### Frequently Asked Questions

- What is SnapPass AI and how does it work?
- Can I create a passport photo from a regular selfie?
- Are there any limits on the number of photos I can generate?
- How does SnapPass AI ensure that generated photos meet official passport requirements?
- What are the recommended photo guidelines for achieving the best results?
- Can I preview and edit my passport photo before downloading it?
- Is my uploaded photo stored, shared or used for AI training?
- What should I do if my generated passport photo gets rejected?
- Are my photos used for AI training or shared with third parties?

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

An alternative approach would be displaying FAQs on the homepage. However, a dedicated FAQ page provides a cleaner user experience, improves maintainability, and allows the FAQ section to grow as the platform evolves.

**Additional context**

* Add an FAQ link within the footer navigation.
* Create a dedicated route for the FAQ page.
* Ensure accessibility and keyboard navigation support.
* Support both Light and Dark themes.
* Maintain consistency with the existing design language.
* I am a GSSoC 2026 participant and would like to work on this issue if assigned.

title: [BUG] Security: Email Enumeration via Password Reset OTP Verification Endpoint
body:
# Security Vulnerability: Email Enumeration via OTP Verification Endpoint

## Describe the bug

The password reset flow attempts to prevent email enumeration by returning a generic success response from the password reset request endpoint regardless of whether an email exists.

However, the OTP verification endpoint (`POST /verify-otp`) returns different responses depending on whether the email exists, allowing attackers to determine if an email address is registered.

File: auth.controller.js
Function: verifyPasswordResetOtp()

This enables account/email enumeration.

## To Reproduce

1. Submit a password reset request for any email address.
2. Call `POST /verify-otp` with a non-existent email and any OTP value.

Example:

```json
{
  "email": "nonexistent@example.com",
  "otp": "000000"
}
```

3. Observe the response:

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "OTP verified successfully"
}
```

4. Call `POST /verify-otp` with an existing email and an invalid OTP.

Example:

```json
{
  "email": "existing@example.com",
  "otp": "000000"
}
```

5. Observe that the endpoint returns an OTP-related error (e.g. invalid or expired OTP).

6. Compare the responses. The difference reveals whether the email exists in the system.

## Expected behavior

The endpoint should return an indistinguishable response regardless of:

* Whether the email exists.
* Whether the OTP is valid.
* Whether the OTP is expired.

This prevents attackers from determining whether an account is registered.

Example generic response:

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "If the provided information is valid, verification succeeded."
}
```

## Screenshots

Not applicable.

## Desktop (please complete the following information)

* OS: Any
* Browser: Any
* Version: Any

## Additional context

The password reset request endpoint already attempts to prevent email enumeration by returning a generic success response when the user does not exist. However, the OTP verification endpoint reintroduces enumeration through differing success and error responses.

### Security Impact

An attacker can identify registered email addresses by submitting arbitrary OTP values and comparing responses.

### Suggested Fix

Return the same generic response for all verification attempts or defer OTP validation until the final password reset step.

### Severity

Medium

### Component

Authentication / Password Reset Flow

title: [BUG] No DPI/physical-size validation on generated passport photo sheet
body:
*the bug**
The `/generate-sheet` endpoint arranges multiple copies on an A4 canvas, but there's no visible enforcement of print-accurate DPI (e.g. 300 DPI) for standard passport dimensions (35×45mm, 2×2in, etc.). If the canvas is built in arbitrary pixel dimensions, printed photos can come out the wrong physical size — a critical failure for a passport-photo tool.

**To Reproduce**
1. Generate an A4 sheet.
2. Print at 100% scale.
3. Measure printed photo against expected passport-size dimensions.

**Expected behavior**
Sheet generation should fix DPI explicitly and validate that each photo cell maps to the correct physical size for the selected document type/country.

**Component**
Python AI service

Add a dropdown of common document presets (e.g., US Passport, Schengen Visa, Indian Passport, PAN/Aadhaar photo) that auto-configures crop dimensions, aspect ratio, and required background color, reducing manual guesswork for studio operators.

title: [FEATURE]
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The login page currently allows users to submit the form without entering an email address or password. This can lead to unnecessary server requests and creates a poor user experience because users are not informed about missing required fields before submission.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Implement client-side and server-side validation for the login form. The system should check whether the email and password fields are filled before allowing submission and display clear validation messages when required fields are empty.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

* Displaying a generic error message after form submission.
* Disabling the Login button until all required fields are completed.
* Using browser-native form validation attributes.

**Additional context**

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Open the login page.
2. Leave the email and password fields empty.
3. Click the Login button.

**Expected Behavior**
The system should prevent submission and display validation errors.

**Actual Behavior**
The form is submitted even when required fields are empty.

**Environment**

* Browser: Chrome
* Device: Desktop

**Impact**
Poor user experience and unnecessary server requests.

title: [FEATURE] Manual Edge Touch-up Brush (Mask Editor)
body: **Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.** While our automated background removal (`rembg`) is highly accurate, complex areas like frizzy hair, glasses, or clothing items that blend with the background can sometimes result in jagged edges or accidental cut-offs. Users currently have no way to fix these minor automated imperfections. **Describe the solution you'd like** Add a manual pixel-level edit brush directly in the photo workspace after the background has been processed. Requirements: 1. **Brush Toggles:** Provide an **"Erase"** brush (to remove remaining stray pixels or background artifacts) and a **"Restore"** brush (to bring back parts of the original image that the AI accidentally clipped). 2. **Brush Customization:** A simple range slider to adjust the brush size (diameter). 3. **Canvas Implementation:** Implement this client-side using HTML5 Canvas context methods (`globalCompositeOperation = 'destination-out'` for erasing). **Describe alternatives you've considered** Leaving it entirely to the automated backend tool, but giving users manual precision control ensures a 100% success rate for printing sheets. **Additional context** - This feature will modify the core image workflow inside the `frontend` editor component. - Ensure the changes made by the brush persist when generating the final A4 printable sheet layout.

title: [FEATURE] AI Formal Attire Swapper (Virtual Dressing Room)
body: **Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.** Many users take quick passport photos at home while wearing casual clothes (hoodies, t-shirts, pajamas). However, official passport and visa guidelines strictly require formal or professional attire, leading to photo rejections. **Describe the solution you'd like** We want to introduce a "Virtual Attire Swapper" feature in the editor panel. This will allow users to overlay a clean, transparent PNG template of formal clothes (e.g., a formal suit, blazer, or collared shirt) over their shoulders. Requirements: 1. **Attire Selection UI:** A simple slider/carousel showing available formal clothing templates (Male/Female/Unisex options). 2. **Frontend Canvas Manipulation:** Once selected, the clothing asset should render on top of the user's photo using an HTML5 canvas layer. 3. **Adjustment Controls:** Provide basic controls (or direct drag-and-resize/keyboard arrow handles) so users can scale, rotate, and reposition the suit perfectly over their shoulders. **Describe alternatives you've considered** Using generative AI models (like Stable Diffusion / Inpainting) on the backend is an alternative, but a client-side transparent PNG overlay canvas approach is faster, completely free, and highly reliable for a smooth open-source contribution. **Additional context** - This feature will primarily live in the `frontend` workspace. - Assets for the transparent formal clothing PNGs can be stored in the public directory.

title: bug: POST /api/process returns 404 Not Found on image processing request
body:
When trying to process an uploaded photo in the Editor page, the frontend attempts to send a POST request to the backend processing API, but the server responds with a 404 (Not Found) status code. This prevents the AI-generated result from being generated and breaks the core functionality of the application.

Steps to reproduce
Navigate to the /editor page (snap-pass-ai-rho.vercel.app/editor).

Upload an image under the "Original" section.

Choose a background color (e.g., Off-White) and select a photo size preset.

Trigger the AI processing action.

See the UI error message: "Request failed with status code 404".

Expected behavior
The frontend should successfully communicate with the backend endpoint (https://snappass-ai.onrender.com/api/process), process the image, and render the resulting AI-generated preview in the "Processed" box.

Screenshots / Console Logs
Console Error Logs:

Plaintext
POST https://snappass-ai.onrender.com/api/process 404 (Not Found)

Error: Request failed with status code 404
    at api-CPz_U5ju.js:9:5850
    at async $.request (api-CPz_U5ju.js:5:2118)
    at async r (photoService-paREd7u0.js:1:277)
    at async EditorPage-DIY5wgX1.js:1:6798
Possible Root Causes
Incorrect Base URL / Missing Prefix: The backend route might be configured differently on Render (e.g., it might actually be listening on https://snappass-ai.onrender.com/process without the /api prefix, or /api/v1/process).

Deployment Sync Issue: The backend deployment on Render might be failing or may not have the /api/process route merged and deployed yet.

CORS / Routing Misconfiguration: The server might be rejecting or failing to route the request correctly due to an unhandled trailing slash or route parameter mismatch.

title: Security Enhancement: Restrict Admin Dashboard Access with Authentication
body: The Admin Dashboard can currently be accessed directly from the navigation bar without any authentication or authorization check. Administrative panels should be protected and accessible only to authorized users. Unauthenticated visitors should be redirected to a login page or shown an access denied message. Current Behavior User clicks the Admin button in the navbar. Admin Dashboard opens immediately. No login, authentication, or role verification is required. Expected Behavior Users should be required to authenticate before accessing the Admin Dashboard. Only accounts with admin privileges should be allowed access. Unauthorized users should be redirected to a login page or receive an appropriate access denied message. Benefits Improves application security. Prevents unauthorized access to administrative features. Aligns with standard authentication and role-based access control practices. Makes the application production-ready. Suggested Implementation Add Admin Login page. Implement authentication middleware/route protection. Add role-based access control (RBAC). Redirect unauthorized users to login. Screenshot:

title: [BUG]
body: ## Describe the bug: Several text elements and labels within the photo generator panel are highly illegible when the UI switches to a dark theme. Specifically, the field headers ("BACKGROUND COLOUR", "PHOTO SIZE PRESET"), the instruction subtext ("Select the country/visa standard that matches your requirement."), and the file metadata labels ("File", "Size") use a very light blue/grey font color that completely blends into the light-grey background container, violating basic accessibility and contrast standards. ## To Reproduce 1. Steps to reproduce the behaviour: 2. Open the Passport Size Photo Generator tool. 3. Ensure the dark theme/mode is active. 4. Observe the configuration panel on the right side. 5. See that the section headings, instruction text, and file details are nearly invisible against the background. ## Expected behaviour Text elements should maintain an adequate contrast ratio against their background. When in dark theme, text colors for headings, instructions, and labels should be adjusted to darker, highly visible tones (e.g., a deep slate, dark grey, or charcoal) to ensure clear readability against the light-grey card container. ## Screenshot: ## Desktop: - OS: Linux (Ubuntu) - Browser: chrome ### Additional context The contrast issue seems to stem from a partial dark theme implementation where the background container element remained light grey, but the text labels were styled with a light color intended for a dark background, resulting in a "light text on a light background" conflict.

title: [FEATURE] : AI-Powered Background Removal & Replacement using Segmentation Model
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Current background handling likely relies on manual cropping or basic color thresholding, which fails on images with uneven lighting, shadows, or cluttered backgrounds — leading to non-compliant passport photos for many real-world user submissions.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Integrate a lightweight pretrained segmentation model (e.g., U2-Net or MediaPipe Selfie Segmentation) to accurately detect the subject and automatically remove/replace the background with compliant solid colors (white/blue/grey) per passport photo standards.

Proposed implementation:
- Run inference server-side (Python/FastAPI) or client-side (TensorFlow.js/WASM) depending on architecture
- Add edge refinement (alpha matting) to avoid harsh cutouts around hair/glasses
- Validate background color/brightness against country-specific requirements after replacement
- Fallback to manual crop tool when model confidence is low

Acceptance criteria:
- >95% edge accuracy on test images
- Processing time under 2s per image
- Works across varied skin tones, hair types, and lighting conditions
- Configurable target background color per country preset

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
Rule-based/threshold background removal — simpler but fails on complex backgrounds and uneven lighting; manual-only cropping — accurate but slow and not scalable for bulk use.

**Additional context**
This would meaningfully improve photo compliance rates and reduce manual correction effort for users submitting photos with non-ideal backgrounds.

title: [BUG] GET /api/upload/:id route conflicts with POST /api/upload — Multer middleware runs on GET requests causing unexpected errors
body:
In backend/src/routes/upload.routes.js, the upload route file likely mounts Multer middleware at the router level (or on the base path) rather than scoping it only to the POST /api/upload handler. This means when GET /api/upload/:id is called to fetch upload metadata, Multer's file-handling middleware still executes on the incoming GET request — which has no multipart body. Depending on the Multer configuration, this either throws an unhandled error (MulterError: Unexpected field) or silently passes with req.file being undefined, causing the controller to crash when it tries to read req.file.path or similar.
Steps to reproduce:

Start the backend
Successfully upload a photo via POST /api/upload and note the returned id
Call GET /api/upload/<id> to retrieve the upload metadata

Expected behavior: Returns the upload metadata JSON for the given ID.
Actual behavior: Multer middleware fires on the GET request, either throwing a MulterError or crashing the controller due to req.file being undefined.

title: [Feature]: Add Behavioral Anomaly Detection for Suspicious Password Access Patterns
body:
🔵 Title : **Add Behavioral Anomaly Detection for Suspicious Password Access Patterns**

🔵  Problem Statement

Currently, SnapPass focuses on secure password sharing with features like one-time access and expiration. However, the system lacks any mechanism to detect suspicious or abnormal access behavior.

For example:

* Multiple rapid access attempts from different IPs
* Access from unusual geolocations
* Automated/script-based retrieval patterns
* Repeated failed access attempts

These scenarios may indicate malicious activity, but the system currently treats all access requests uniformly without any intelligence layer.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

🔵 Why this is important

Without anomaly detection:

* Potential brute-force or scraping attempts go unnoticed
* No alerting or mitigation mechanism exists
* Security is purely reactive rather than proactive

Adding behavioral monitoring would significantly enhance the security model and make SnapPass more robust for real-world usage.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

🔵 Proposed Solution

Introduce a lightweight anomaly detection system that:

1. Tracks access patterns:

   * IP frequency
   * Access timestamps
   * Request intervals
   * Failed vs successful attempts

2. Implements rule-based + ML-based detection:

   * Threshold-based alerts (e.g., >X requests/min)
   * Optional anomaly detection model (Isolation Forest / statistical methods)

3. Adds response mechanisms:

   * Temporary blocking of suspicious IPs
   * Rate limiting
   * Alert logs or warnings

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

🔵  Possible Implementation Approach

* Maintain request logs in Redis (already used in project)
* Add middleware layer to monitor access behavior
* Use simple statistical anomaly detection initially (z-score / thresholding)
* Extend to ML-based detection as optional enhancement

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

🔵 Expected Outcome

* Improved security against automated/malicious access
* Better real-world usability
* Foundation for advanced security features

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

🔵 Additional Context

This feature aligns well with modern secure-sharing systems, where behavioral intelligence is increasingly used alongside encryption to prevent misuse.

title: [FEATURE] Add Cookies Policy Page and Cookie Consent Popup
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently, the application does not provide users with information about how cookies are used, stored, or managed. Additionally, users are not asked for consent before non-essential cookies are utilized. This may reduce transparency and can make it difficult for users to understand the site's data collection practices.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Implement a dedicated **Cookies Policy Page** that clearly explains:

* What cookies are and why they are used.
* Types of cookies used by the application (essential, analytics, functional, etc.).
* Cookie retention periods.
* How users can manage or disable cookies.
* Contact information for privacy-related concerns.

Additionally, add a **Cookie Consent Popup/Banner** that:

* Appears when a user visits the site for the first time.
* Briefly explains cookie usage.
* Provides options such as:

  * Accept All Cookies
  * Reject Non-Essential Cookies
  * Customize Preferences
* Stores the user's consent preferences locally.
* Includes a link to the Cookies Policy Page.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

* Adding a short cookie notice only in the footer.
* Including cookie-related information solely within the Privacy Policy page.

While these approaches provide some information, a dedicated Cookies Policy page combined with a consent popup offers better transparency, user control, and a more professional user experience.

**Additional context**

Suggested implementation details:

* Add a new route/page for the Cookies Policy.
* Include a footer link directing users to the page.
* Make the consent banner responsive across desktop and mobile devices.
* Persist user preferences using local storage or a similar mechanism.
* Ensure the popup follows accessibility best practices, including keyboard navigation and screen-reader support.

This feature would improve user trust, transparency, and compliance with modern privacy standards.

title: [FEATURE] : Enhanced Footer with Contact Form & Cursor Glow Toggle
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The current footer provides navigation links and a support email address, but users do not have a direct way to contact the team without opening their email client. Additionally, while the application is functional and clean, it lacks interactive UI elements that can enhance the overall user experience and give the product a more modern, premium feel.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Enhance the footer section by introducing the following improvements:

📩 Contact Us Form

Add a responsive contact form within the footer that includes:

- Name field
- Email field
- Message field
- Submit button

The form should include validation and provide clear success/error feedback to users.

✨ Cursor Glow Effect

Implement a subtle cursor-following glow effect that enhances the visual experience on desktop devices.

Requirements:

- Smooth animation
- Lightweight and performance-friendly
- Theme-aware glow colors
- Disabled or optimized for mobile devices

🎛️ Cursor Glow Toggle

Add an ON/OFF toggle for the cursor glow effect, preferably within an Appearance or Settings section.

Suggested behavior:

- Users can enable or disable the effect at any time
- Preference is saved using localStorage
- Works across page reloads

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

- Keeping only the support email address.
- Creating a separate Contact page instead of a footer contact form.
- Using only hover animations or background gradients instead of a cursor glow effect.
- Implementing the glow effect without a toggle.

While these alternatives are viable, integrating a contact form directly into the footer and providing a customizable cursor glow effect offers a better balance between usability, accessibility, and visual appeal.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

- Keeping only the support email address.
- Adding a separate Contact page instead of a footer form.
- Using a modal popup for contact submissions.

While these alternatives are viable, a footer-integrated contact form provides the most accessible and convenient experience without requiring users to navigate away from the current page.

**Additional context**

The footer currently contains product links, company information, and contact details. Expanding it with a contact form would improve communication and user engagement, while the cursor glow effect would enhance the premium feel of the application. Together, these changes would modernize the footer experience while maintaining SnapPass AI's clean and professional design.
